As we can see, even the smallest of these companies is several times larger than RGC Resources. This may discourage some investors from purchasing the company’s shares, but there is no reason to avoid a company just because it is relatively small. RGC Resources is still quite liquid, as more than 10,000 shares trade hands on most days:

As such, this should still be a stock that any investor can get in and out of quickly, and unless that investor is trading tens of thousands of dollars in a single trade, it should not move the price too much. At the current price of $22.45 per share, 10,000 shares is $224,500, which is far larger than most individual investors conduct in a single transaction. With that said, though, this daily volume could be a problem for institutional investors, but it should not be problematic for individuals, especially individuals who can spread their purchases out with small trades over a period of a few days. As such, despite the small size of the company, it should be liquid enough for an average person.

The small size of the company could make it too small for institutional investors, however, and this might have actually given it an advantage in the recent market volatility. This chart shows the stock price of RGC Resources against the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past month:

As we can see, RGC Resources was not affected much by the crash in the prices of most common stocks in response to President Trump’s tariff announcement. One reason for this is that much of the sell-off was caused by leveraged institutional investors (such as hedge funds) dumping stock indiscriminately. A utility company as small as RGC Resources is likely to have many of its shares in the hands of long-term individual holders, who are less likely to have to dump the stock because of margin calls and are less likely to dump just because of a short-term market panic. Thus, RGC Resources might be attractive to a retiree or other long-term holders who are looking to own something relatively safe as a core position in their portfolio.

Speaking of the dividend, RGC Resources is profitable and pays its shareholders a very respectable dividend. As of the time of writing, the yield of the company’s stock is 3.70%, which is substantially higher than the yield of any of the major domestic equity indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.31%

NASDAQ 100 Index (QQQ)

0.64%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.30%

Some readers might note that these yields are a lot lower than the figures that I provided in an article yesterday. This stands as evidence of how powerfully the market rallied, as it was sufficient to reduce the yields of the major indices by close to ten basis points. In any case, we can see that the yield of RGC Resources is far lower than that of the major market indices, which should be somewhat attractive to anyone who is seeking to earn some income off of their portfolios.

The current yield of RGC Resources is competitive with its peers, but the company is not the highest-yielding one in the space. Here is how RGC Resources compares with its peers:

Company Name

Current Yield

RGC Resources

3.70%

Southwest Gas Holdings

3.53%

ONE Gas

3.65%

Atmos Energy

2.34%

NiSource

2.94%

Northwest Natural Holding

4.85%

New Jersey Resources

3.79%

As we can see here, the current yield of RGC Resources is actually lower than that of New Jersey Resources and particularly Northwest Natural Holding. It is slightly higher than most of the others, however. This might suggest that the market feels that RGC Resources has greater growth potential than Northwest Natural Holding, since it will need to deliver a higher level of capital appreciation in order to deliver a yield that is competitive with its peer. However, it might also be the case that the market is overvaluing RGC Resources for some reason. This is something that we should investigate further.

Recent Performance

I last discussed RGC Resources on November 8, 2023, so about seventeen months have passed since that time. The company's stock performance since that time has been quite good, as it has gained 39.01%, beating the U.S. Utilities Index (IDU) and the S&P 500 Index:

However, we can see that it did appear to have a bit more volatility than the indices, which might be due to the fact that this company has a substantially lower volume than the average large-cap stocks that comprise both indices. This recent volatility might not be what some investors want to see, especially risk-averse retirees and others who are looking at utility stocks, but the outperformance versus the indices is certainly appreciated.

About RGC Resources

As mentioned in the introduction, RGC Resources is a relatively small regulated natural gas utility that serves customers in the greater Roanoke Valley. This is a region of Southwest Virginia that borders the Roanoke River, which would also be considered part of Appalachia:

The company, curiously, does not include a map of its service territory on its website or in any of its investor presentations. The map above shows the region that the Roanoke Valley Association of Realtors considers to be the greater Roanoke Valley region, but this does not necessarily mean that RGC Resources’ infrastructure actually covers this entire five-county area. In fact, it almost certainly does not, given the company’s customer count. However, since the company does not provide a map itself, this is the best information that we have available to us. The company’s service territory is located within the five-county region shown above, particularly the area of Roanoke City and Roanoke County.

The company’s most recent 10-Q filing (linked earlier) states that it has a customer count of 63,400, which is actually a bit less than the 95,400 people who live in Roanoke City alone. This is not particularly surprising, though, considering that a household with multiple people in it will not have multiple natural gas utility accounts. This is the highest figure that the company has ever had in terms of customer count:

This is interesting considering that the city of Roanoke itself is suffering from a population decline. According to the World Population Review, the city’s population is currently declining at a 0.94% rate. The cumulative population decline since the 2020 census is estimated at 4.54%. This is not expected to change anytime soon. As we can see here, the city’s population is expected to have declined to approximately 91,700 people by 2029 compared to 95,400 people today:

This could certainly create headwinds on the company’s growth, although it has done fairly well at sustaining its customer growth so far. Normally, the population decline would be problematic since anyone who leaves the company’s service territory, for whatever reason, can obviously no longer be a customer of it. However, there are two things that we need to keep in mind when looking at this chart. The first is that the company explicitly states that it serves the greater Roanoke Valley, although it does not specifically state which parts of the region it serves. The population projection above is for the city of Roanoke itself, which is the most important city in the region, but not the entire region. If some of the population decline that we see is caused by people leaving the city and settling in the suburbs, they might not be leaving the company’s service territory entirely. A second consideration is that when an individual moves out of a shared household and gets their own place, it can actually provide a boost to the company’s customer count (since an additional household is being served) even in the face of a declining population. Thus, the declining population might not be as big of a headwind as it would at first appear.

There actually could be some evidence that individuals are leaving Roanoke City but remaining within the county. The World Population Review states that Roanoke County is growing its population at a 0.28% growth rate and is expected to continue to grow going forward:

The World Population Review puts the current population of the county at 97,556 people, which is surprisingly not much more than the city itself. The population of Roanoke County is expected to reach 98,600 by 2029. This is still not a high number, especially not when compared to many of the other utilities that we have discussed in this column, but at least it is some growth.

With that said, the population of Roanoke City is expected to decline by 3,700 people through 2029. The population growth of Roanoke County will not fully offset that, as the county is expected to only add approximately 1,044 people over the same period. As such, the demographics of the region could very well be a headwind here.

While the forward demographics of Roanoke City and Roanoke County overall do not look too promising, RGC Resources states in its most recent investor presentation that it has been making an effort to expand its operations into nearby Franklin County. Franklin County is visible on the map above and is located to the south and southeast of Roanoke. Franklin County has very positive demographics, as it also has a growing population. The current population of the county is estimated at 56,489 people, and it is growing at a 0.84% annual rate. This growth is likewise expected to continue:

This estimate puts the expected population of Franklin County at approximately 58,400 people by 2029, an increase of 1,911 people over today’s levels. However, this could be an even bigger growth opportunity for RGC Resources than this figure suggests. This is because the company’s presence in this area is very limited right now. As such, RGC Resources appears to have an opportunity here to connect people to its system who formerly had no natural gas service at all. As such, the company’s opportunity for customer growth in Franklin County is far more than what the population growth alone suggests. Some of the company’s management’s comments suggest that they see this as one of its biggest near-term growth opportunities, and indeed that may be correct.

There is a great deal of evidence that suggests that potential customers are willing to switch over to natural gas whenever it becomes available in their areas. The U.S. Department of Energy reports that in most cases, it is significantly cheaper to heat a home with natural gas than with any other commonly used heating fuel. This fact has certainly not been lost on homeowners, as the Department of Energy reports that one customer every minute connects their property to the infrastructure of a natural gas utility:

As such, RGC Resources should have little difficulty connecting new customers in Franklin County to its utility service. As it expands its distribution network to new housing developments (which it is working to achieve) then it is likely to see people signing up for service and increasing its customer count. This provides a solid avenue for growth in the face of the rather weak demographics in Roanoke City and Roanoke County.

Rate Base Expansion

RGC Resources is also working to increase its rate base as another avenue to generate growth. This should allow it to generate some growth through raising prices on its existing customers, and thus allowing it to grow revenue at a faster rate than it adds new customers.

Unlike most utilities, RGC Resources has not provided a multi-year capital spending plan to its investors. Normally, such a multi-year plan would give investors an idea of what the company’s rate-based growth is likely to be going forward. It also gives us an idea of what the company’s financing needs might be, which could be important if it is going to raise capital through the issuance of new equity. For example, peer company ONE Gas is likely to dilute its shareholders because it will be issuing equity capital to finance its rate base growth over the coming years. Naturally, such a move reduces a company’s forward earnings per share growth, so it would be nice to know if RGC Resources is planning this so that we can adjust our return expectations accordingly.

So far, the only capital investment guidance that RGC Resources has provided shareholders is that it intends to invest approximately $21.7 million into its infrastructure in 2025:

This projection includes $2.8 million into the Mountain Valley Pipeline, which is intended to increase the capacity of that system. RGC Resources only owns 0.73% of that pipeline, so obviously this investment is only a fraction of the total amount needed to complete the capacity expansion of that project. However, the remainder of that needed capital investment will be done by the other partners to the project and are not relevant for our discussion of RGC Resources today.

The actual amount that RGC Resources will be investing in its natural gas utility appears to be approximately $18.9 million this year. That is pretty similar to the amount that the company spent in 2024 and down a bit from 2022 and 2023 levels:

The company grew its rate base at an 8.4% compound annual growth rate over the 2010 to 2024 period. As its 2025 projected spending is in line with the 2024 level, we can expect its rate base growth to be slightly lower because the depreciation of its assets will likely be higher in 2025 than in 2024. After all, the company’s depreciation expense increases every year:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

This is completely normal for a utility and nothing that we need to be concerned about. It does mean, however, that the company would actually need to increase its capital spending every year in order to overcome the rising depreciation and still grow its rate base at the same growth rate. As the company’s planned capital spending in 2025 is in line with 2024, rather than increasing, we can expect its rate base growth to be a little lower than in 2024.

Financial Considerations

As is the case with most utilities and other capital-intensive companies, RGC Resources has seen its interest expenses rise over the past few years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As clearly shown, the company’s total and net interest expenses came in at $1.8 million for the most recent quarter, which in the case of RGC Resources was the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year (RGC Resources has an October to September fiscal year). This represents a 63.64% increase over the $1.1 million that the company spent on debt service during the third quarter of 2022 (the three-month period that ended on June 30, 2022). While this is certainly a fairly large increase over an eleven-quarter period, it was not ridiculously out of line with the increases that its peers reported over the same period:

Company Name

Increase in Total Interest Expense Over Period

Increase in Net Interest Expense Over Period

RGC Resources

63.64%

63.64%

Southwest Gas Holdings

38.91%

36.00%

ONE Gas

144.17%

144.17%

Atmos Energy

101.91%

101.91%

NiSource

116.57%

115.02%

Northwest Natural Holding

117.24%

70.69%

New Jersey Resources

58.41%

53.62%

(figures used to perform the calculations were obtained from Seeking Alpha)

We can see that all of these peer companies saw both their net and total interest expenses increase by quite a lot over the period in question. Southwest Gas was the only exception to this rule, as it managed to limit the amount by which its interest expenses rose over the period. In this light, RGC Resources actually did not do too poorly, as its increase in debt servicing costs was lower than four of the six peer companies in the chart. This is a pretty good situation, overall, especially when we consider that rising interest expenses are a drag on earnings growth.

It unfortunately does appear to be the case that RGC Resources’ rising interest expenses have been a drag on earnings growth. In many cases, we do not mind an increase in interest expenses as long as the company’s revenues rise more rapidly. That situation means that the company is taking on debts to fund projects that generate sufficient revenue to support themselves. We do not see that when we look at RGC Resources’ revenue growth over the period shown above:

(figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As shown, RGC Resources’ total revenues went from $17.3 million to $27.3 million over the eleven-quarter period. That is only a 57.80% increase, which was lower than what the company’s debt service increase came in at. This tells us that the rising interest expenses have been a drag on the company’s ability to grow its net income and earnings per share. It also tells us that the rising interest expenses have been a headwind for the company’s ability to grow its dividend. After all, a greater proportion of the company’s revenues are needed to service its debt than was the case two years ago. This is a very disappointing situation, and it could be problematic for anyone who is seeking a reliable source of income from their assets.

Valuation

Zacks Investment Research states that RGC Resources is currently trading with a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 17.80. This is better than the 20.61 forward price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Utilities Sector. Here is how it compares to the company’s peer group:

Company Name

Forward P/E Ratio

RGC Resources

17.80

Southwest Gas Holdings

18.13

ONE Gas

16.86

Atmos Energy

20.46

NiSource

19.80

Northwest Natural Holding

14.28

New Jersey Resources

14.95

(all figures from Zacks Investment Research)

These figures suggest that RGC Resources is at best fairly valued at its current share price. It is the median stock shown in this comparison of other natural gas utilities, so it is hard to make a case that it is undervalued at the current price.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant System appears to agree, as it has assigned a C- valuation grade to the stock at the current price:

This, unfortunately, does not compare very well with the company’s peers:

Company Name

Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation Grade

RGC Resources

C-

Southwest Gas Holdings

B-

ONE Gas

C

Atmos Energy

D-

NiSource

D

Northwest Natural Holding

B+

New Jersey Resources

C+

While not the worst-valued company in the peer group, it is obviously very difficult to make a case for RGC Resources being attractively valued compared to its peers. After all, we see four companies that have more attractive valuations using this system to evaluate them.

Overall, RGC Resources is at best fairly valued right now. It is certainly not undervalued and as such, it may make sense for any investor to wait for a more attractive entry point. The company’s stock is down midday on April 10, so it is possible that a more attractive entry point will present itself in the near future if this continues.

Conclusion

In conclusion, RGC Resources is a small and very underfollowed natural gas utility serving Roanoke and the surrounding area in southwestern Virginia. The fact that the company is much smaller than many of the other utilities that we have discussed in the past is not necessarily a problem, though, as a small company can earn positive net income and cash flow just as well as a large one can. RGC Resources proves this, as the company boasts a fairly impressive 3.70% distribution yield and some growth prospects as it expands into another county near Roanoke.

However, it is difficult to make a case for purchasing the company at its current valuation. It does not appear to be undervalued right now, and several of its peers are more attractively priced. If today’s share price weakness continues going forward, though, then it might start to look attractive. As such, investors who are looking for a safe haven to ride out the market carnage might want to start watching it.
